ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Yong Tau Foo or mala ban sauce combination. FOODON_03302050 (tofu-based food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Tofu and tofu products) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Yong Tau Foo or mala ban sauce composite dish in FoodEx2 catalogue.

main_ingredients: mala sauce (chilli oil, Sichuan peppercorn, dried chilli, garlic, sesame)

serving.description: 8 pieces of Yong Tau Foo with mala ban sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving consists of 8 pieces of assorted Yong Tau Foo items (approximately 320 g total) tossed in mala ban sauce, typically consumed as a main meal.

nutrient_source.title: Yong Tau Foo (mixed items, with sauce) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the exact composite dish of 8-piece Yong Tau Foo with mala ban sauce. Values are estimated by combining HPB Singapore Foodo data for Yong Tau Foo (mixed items) with USDA FoodData Central data for mala/chilli oil sauce components. Per-100g values represent a weighted average of the assembled dish at approximately 320 g serving. Core macros are cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN and FAO/INFOODS SEA regional analogues for plausibility.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed tofu and fish paste dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu-based dishes and mixed Asian composite meals
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo with mala ban sauce. GI of 48 is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu-based dishes (GI ~40–50) and mixed Asian composite meals with moderate carbohydrate content. The mala ban sauce contributes negligible carbohydrate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 29.4) / 100 ≈ 14.1, rounded to 14.2. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from mala sauce, fish paste, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 4–5 pieces and request reduced sauce, as a full 8-piece serving with mala ban sauce may contribute approximately 1,344 mg sodium, representing over 55% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load and carbohydrate content from tofu items and sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of approximately 48 and a glycemic load of ~14 per serving, this dish is moderately suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without additional rice or noodles; portion control and monitoring of the mala sauce (which may contain added sugar) are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Chilli oil and saturated fat in mala sauce; sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The mala ban sauce contributes chilli oil and moderate saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request lighter sauce application and limit frequency of consumption to manage cumulative sodium and fat intake, while benefiting from the soy protein and fish paste components which provide heart-healthy protein.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo with Mala Ban Sauce (8 pieces) is a popular Singaporean-Malaysian dish consisting of eight assorted stuffed tofu and fish paste items — typically including firm tofu, soft tofu, eggplant, bitter gourd, okra, chilli, and fish balls — tossed in a mala ban (麻辣拌) dry sauce. The mala ban sauce is a Sichuan-influenced spicy and numbing condiment made from chilli oil, Sichuan peppercorn, dried chilli, garlic, sesame paste, and sesame seeds, adapted for the Singaporean palate. Unlike the traditional Yong Tau Foo served in soup or with a sweet sauce, the mala ban variant is served dry-tossed, giving each piece a bold, aromatic coating. The texture is a contrast of soft, yielding tofu and fish paste with a slightly chewy exterior from frying or steaming. Nutritionally, this dish provides a moderate protein source from fish paste and tofu (approximately 7.8 g protein per 100 g), with a relatively low glycemic index (~48) making it a reasonable choice for blood glucose management when eaten without additional starchy sides. However, the sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1,344 mg per 8-piece serving) due to the mala sauce and seasoning, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and contains soy, fish, and sesame allergens. It is commonly consumed as a main meal in Singapore hawker centres and food delivery platforms.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the exact composite dish of 8-piece Yong Tau Foo with mala ban sauce. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo Yong Tau Foo data combined with USDA FoodData Central mala/chilli oil sauce components, cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN and FAO/INFOODS SEA regional analogues. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and multi-source, but direct measurement data for this specific composite dish is not available in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo (Yong Tau Foo mixed items) and USDA FoodData Central (mala sauce components); no single approved-source entry covers this exact composite dish. Values should be updated if a direct HPB or IMCR-NIN measurement becomes available. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA source data for this dish type. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this dish. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this dish. 5. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database tofu-based dish entries; no direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo with mala ban sauce. 6. Halal status is listed as false because mala ban sauce from some vendors may contain non-halal ingredients; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace per USDA FoodData Central data for equivalent sauce and tofu components; this is a confirmed trace value consistent with minimally processed tofu and fish paste items.
